stories.title: Prologis Acquires 26 Acres in Glendale Heights for 454K-SF Development
stories.excerpt: Prologis has acquired 26.19 acres of land in Glendale Heights, comprising 25.56 acres at 375 Army Trail Road and a 0.6-acre portion of 400 Regency Drive. Plans call for the speculative development of two logistics facilities totaling 454,000 square feet, making it the current largest new development in the submarket’s history. Each building will offer 36-foot clear heights, over 200 car parking spaces and a shared 200-foot truck court. The site is located in an established industrial area, adjacent to the High Grove Business Park to the east and a commercial and retail development with nearby amenities to the west. The property offers direct access to I-355 and proximity to Chicago O’Hare International Airport and the broader Chicago metropolitan area. “This is a pivotal transaction for Prologis as we build out our active development pipeline across Chicago,” said Josh Bauer, vice president and investment officer for Prologis. “Unlocking an opportunity of this scale in Central DuPage is extremely rare, and we look forward to advancing a development that reflects the priorities of Glendale Heights and will support hundreds of businesses and help strengthen the region’s supply chain.” The post Prologis Acquires 26 Acres in Glendale Heights for 454K-SF Development appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Prologis acquires 26.19 acres in Glendale Heights, IL for 454K-SF logistics development
Two speculative industrial buildings with 36-ft clear heights, positioned near I-355 and O'Hare
Largest new development in Central DuPage submarket; rare land assemblage opportunity
stories.summary: Prologis closed on a rare 26-acre parcel in Glendale Heights, IL to develop two speculative logistics buildings totaling 454,000 square feet with 36-foot clear heights and 200-foot truck court. The site sits in an industrial park adjacent to I-355 with proximity to O'Hare, making it the largest new development in the Central DuPage submarket. The deal signals strong conviction in Chicago's industrial/logistics demand and a tight land market.
stories.impact: This Prologis move underscores robust e-commerce and supply-chain-driven demand in Chicago's western suburbs and signals tight industrial land availability. Multifamily developers should note that logistics capital flowing into DuPage County (I-355 corridor) typically precedes or runs parallel to workforce housing demand; JPI, Meritage, and other resi builders should monitor jobs and population shifts into Glendale Heights and nearby submarkets for build opportunities.

stories.title: Klyde Warren Park expansion set to bring $4B in real estate value to Dallas
stories.excerpt: Klyde Warren Park, which marks the transition from Downtown Dallas to Uptown, is set to expand by nearly two acres. The park’s chairman, Jody Grant, said that the 1.7 acre expansion of the park could create around $3.5 billion in new real estate value in the area via investment and tourism, according to the Dallas Business Journal. Grant also claims that the park has added $5.9 billion in real estate value for the Arts District and Uptown. The added value has filled the pockets of the city, Dallas County, and DISD to the tune of $957 million in incremental tax […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Klyde Warren Park 1.7-acre expansion projected to generate $3.5B in new real estate value
Park chairman claims cumulative $5.9B in value added to Arts District/Uptown; $957M in tax revenue
24K-SF event space + Jacobs Lawn + seasonal ice rink; Greystar building 896-unit apartment on former Sears/JCPenney
stories.summary: Klyde Warren Park's 1.7-acre westward expansion and new event/retail pavilion are expected to catalyze $3.5 billion in new real estate investment across Dallas's Arts District and Uptown. The park itself has contributed $5.9 billion in cumulative value and $957 million in tax revenue. Concurrent with this, Greystar is delivering an 896-unit apartment complex on former department store parcels at nearby Miami International Mall in Doral.
stories.impact: Klyde Warren's expansion is a major demand signal for multifamily in Uptown and Arts District—the park creates a walkable, high-amenity anchor that draws residents and employers. For TCR: the park acts as a placemaking catalyst that de-risks multifamily development in adjacent submarkets; the $3.5B in projected value suggests strong absorption potential for Class A resi projects within a 0.5–1 mile radius, especially along McKinney Ave and in the Core/Uptown submarkets.

stories.title: New Tenants Now Fully Occupy Former 3.5M-SF Shreveport GM Plant
stories.excerpt: Industrial Realty Group, LLC (IRG) announced that its tenant, SLB expanded its operations at IRG’s Shreveport Business Park campus, occupying a total of 3.1 million square feet, taking the balance of the remaining space at the former General Motors plant. The milestone marks the culmination of a transformation for the 3.5-million-square-foot industrial campus. SLB, Hyundai Glovis, and USPS also operate on site. SLB first announced plans to establish operations at the Shreveport campus in 2023, leasing more than 1 million square feet for advanced manufacturing operations. The project represented an $18.5 million investment. The most recent expansion came with an additional $30 million investment. By 2027, an estimated 1,400 employees will work at Shreveport Business Park, well-surpassing the approximately 800 employees working there at the time of GM’s 2012 closure. The post New Tenants Now Fully Occupy Former 3.5M-SF Shreveport GM Plant appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: SLB fully occupies remaining 3.1M SF at former Shreveport GM plant; 3.5M-SF total now leased
SLB invested $48.5M total ($18.5M + $30M in two phases) in advanced manufacturing operations
By 2027, Shreveport Business Park will employ 1,400 vs. ~800 at GM closure in 2012
stories.summary: SLB (Schlumberger) has fully leased the Shreveport Business Park, a former 3.5-million-square-foot General Motors manufacturing plant, with two tranches of investment totaling $48.5 million. The expansion transforms the property into a multi-tenant industrial campus (SLB, Hyundai Glovis, USPS also on-site) and is projected to employ 1,400 workers by 2027, nearly double the ~800 jobs when GM exited in 2012.
stories.impact: This signals major job creation in Shreveport and validates the industrial reuse model in secondary markets. For multifamily: 1,400 new advanced-manufacturing jobs in Shreveport creates acute workforce-housing demand in a market with limited new supply. Developers should underwrite entry-level, workforce, and mid-market apartment communities (200–300 units, $700–900/month) within 10–15 miles of Shreveport Business Park; demand is likely to exceed supply given GM's legacy and SLB's stature.

stories.title: Lincoln Property, J.P. Morgan Acquire 962K-SF Wakefield Office Campus
stories.excerpt: Lincoln Property Company, in partnership with investors advised by J.P. Morgan Asset Management, closed on the acquisition of The Edge, a 962,000-square-foot office campus in Wakefield, MA, north of Boston. The seller was Hobbs Brook Real Estate. At one time the site of an amusement park, The Edge is a seven-building campus offering a scalable footprint with significant value-add and mixed-use redevelopment potential. Lincoln and its partner plan to execute a strategic repositioning program designed to enhance long-term competitiveness and create a dynamic live-work-play environment. “The Edge represents a compelling opportunity to acquire a large-scale campus in a fundamentally strong suburban market,” said Scott Faber, SVP at Lincoln Property Company in Boston. “The Edge opportunity compliments Lincoln’s recent office campus investments in urban adjacent neighborhoods within proximity to Boston.” A Newmark team of Rob Griffin, Edward C. Maher, Jr., Jim Tribble, Samantha Hallowell and Will Sleeper represented Hobbs Brook. Terms of the sale were not disclosed. The post Lincoln Property, J.P. Morgan Acquire 962K-SF Wakefield Office Campus appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Lincoln Property + JPMorgan Asset Mgmt acquire 962K-SF office campus (The Edge) in Wakefield, MA
Seven-building suburban Boston campus with mixed-use/live-work-play repositioning strategy
Former amusement park site; significant value-add and redevelopment potential flagged
stories.summary: Lincoln Property Company and JPMorgan Asset Management closed on The Edge, a 962,000-square-foot, seven-building office campus in Wakefield, Massachusetts (north of Boston). The property, a former amusement park, will undergo strategic repositioning to create a live-work-play environment with mixed-use potential. Lincoln has been active in urban-adjacent Boston office repositioning and sees this as a long-term value-add play.
stories.impact: This is a office-to-mixed-use conversion signal in a fundamentally strong suburban Boston market. For multifamily developers: Wakefield and inner-ring Route 128 suburbs are undersupplied relative to job growth (tech, life sciences, biotech concentration). The Edge's repositioning may include resi components; if Lincoln/JPM convert even 30–40% to apartments, that's 200–300 units in a tight labor market. Monitor this deal for ground-lease or conversion-apartment opportunities in the Boston 128 submarket.

stories.title: Rio Capital Purchases 95K-SF Aurora Shopping Center
stories.excerpt: Rio Capital Investments purchased Plaza West at Aurora Town Center, a 95,000 square foot shopping center at 13670 E. Alameda Ave., for $15.7 million. The Denver Business Journal reports its acquisition of Plaza West at Aurora Town Center was aided through a $10.24 million promissory note from United Omaha Life Insurance Company. Plaza West at Aurora Town Center was constructed in 1984 and 1985. It is anchored by Harbor Freight Tools and Dollar Tree. The seller was Arileus Capital. The shopping center last traded hands for $8 million in 2024. At the time of the previous sale, Plaza West was 65% occupied. Prior to selling the property, Arileus fully leased the center. Tenants include Colorado Pro Gym, Tendercare Dental and the third location of King Buffet. Arileus was represented by Justin Krieger, principal at Pinnacle Real Estate Advisors. The post Rio Capital Purchases 95K-SF Aurora Shopping Center appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Rio Capital acquires 95K-SF Plaza West shopping center in Aurora, CO for $15.7M
Property fully leased after Arileus Capital's pre-sale stabilization; prior 2024 sale was $8M at 65% occupancy
Harbor Freight, Dollar Tree anchors; newer tenants include gym, dental, King Buffet
stories.summary: Rio Capital purchased Plaza West at Aurora Town Center, a 95,000-square-foot shopping center, for $15.7 million with $10.24 million in insurance company debt. The property traded in 2024 at $8 million when 65% occupied; the current seller (Arileus Capital) had fully leased the property before sale, validating a value-add strategy. Tenants include Harbor Freight, Dollar Tree, and newer service/food operators.
stories.impact: Limited direct impact on multifamily. This signals that retail-recovery capital is circulating in Denver suburbs (Aurora), and shopping centers at 95% occupancy can achieve ~2x gains in 12 months. Indirectly: retail stabilization in Aurora town centers may support mixed-use development; if Rio or future owners consider mezzanine-level apartments or adjacent parcels, multifamily density could follow. Monitor Aurora I-225 corridors for residential upside, but this deal itself is retail-focused.

stories.title: Mixed-use mania: Mapping mall redevelopments planned in South Florida
stories.excerpt: Many South Florida shopping malls are like chrysalises, struggling to shed their retail-only past and emerge as vibrant mixed-use districts. More than a half-dozen shopping centers across Miami-Dade, Broward and Palm Beach counties are slated for redevelopments to add thousands of apartments alongside retail, restaurants, offices, hotels and entertainment.  The projects reflect the region’s demand for housing and owners’ efforts to revive malls that have lost department stores, grappled with declining foot traffic or struggled to fill vacant space.  But that push for growth hasn’t been without challenges, as several redevelopment plans have encountered political opposition, legal battles or other […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: South Florida mall redevelopments: 6+ projects planned across Miami-Dade, Broward, Palm Beach counties
Greystar 896-unit apartment at Miami International Mall (Doral) on former Sears/JCPenney; Easton Group acquired parcels for $29.3M
Elev8 Fun 110K-SF entertainment center replacing Kohl's building; projects cite declining foot traffic, vacant department stores as catalysts
stories.summary: More than six major shopping centers across South Florida (Miami-Dade, Broward, Palm Beach) are undergoing mixed-use redevelopment with apartments as the primary revenue driver. Greystar's 896-unit project at Miami International Mall in Doral is the flagship, built on former Sears and JCPenney parcels acquired by Easton Group for $29.3 million. Projects also include new entertainment, dining, office, and hotel components to revive retail-only properties losing department store anchors.
stories.impact: Strong signal of adaptive reuse and retail-to-resi conversion momentum in South Florida. For TCR: South Florida's permitting is complex but political will for mixed-use malls is evident. Monitor approval timelines at Doral, Aventura, and Westchester projects; these conversions often require city incentives (TIF, parking waivers, zoning relief) that can unlock value for first-movers. Greystar's 896-unit anchor suggests 600–1,000-unit projects are feasible in these nodes; land cost and entitled density are likely more favorable than greenfield in Miami-Dade.

stories.title: JPI to Start Work on $90M Austin Apartment Project
stories.excerpt: JPI has closed on a 9.32-acre site in Austin, Texas, where it will build Jefferson Pearson Ranch, a $90 million, 342-unit, Class A community in the Pearson Ranch mixed-use development. The Dallas-based company is scheduled to break ground in August, with completion slated for 2028. The project in the 156-acre, 2.6 million-square-foot Pearson Ranch mixed-use development. The community, located at Pearson Market Circle and Spectrum Drive in Northwest Austin, will include a mix of multifamily, hospitality, office, retail, dining and community spaces. Select units will have private patios, balconies or fenced yards, along with mudrooms, flex spaces, dry bars and bathrooms with soaking tubs. Community amenities at Jefferson Pearson Ranch will include a standalone fitness center with indoor and outdoor workout space, a pool with sundecks and a cabana, a resident lounge, a coworking space, a private conference room and whisper rooms, large grills and outdoor kitchens. The post JPI to Start Work on $90M Austin Apartment Project appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: JPI closes 9.32-acre site in Austin for $90M, 342-unit Class A apartment (Jefferson Pearson Ranch)
Groundbreaking August 2026, delivery 2028; part of 156-acre, 2.6M-SF mixed-use Pearson Ranch campus
Northwest Austin location (Pearson Market Circle/Spectrum Drive); high-end finishes, wellness/coworking amenities
stories.summary: JPI acquired a 9.32-acre parcel in Northwest Austin and will develop Jefferson Pearson Ranch, a $90 million, 342-unit Class A apartment community scheduled to break ground in August 2026 and deliver in 2028. The project is anchored within the larger Pearson Ranch mixed-use development (156 acres, 2.6M SF) and will feature high-end finishes, private patios/yards, and amenities including fitness, pool, coworking, and outdoor kitchens.
stories.impact: This is a bullish signal for Northwest Austin supply and pricing power. JPI's $90M for 342 units (~$263K per unit hard cost) reflects strong demand and rising construction costs in Austin's supply-constrained submarket. For TCR: Monitor Pearson Ranch's absorption and rent growth (expected to be Class A+ pricing, $1,800+/month for 1BR); if Jefferson sells out or stabilizes ahead of 2028 schedule, it validates the northwest corridor for additional supply. Strong job growth (Oracle, Tesla, Apple expansion) supports multiple projects in this submarket.

stories.title: Obra Supplies $31M Refi on Arizona Northrop Grumman-Leased Office Property
stories.excerpt: Silver Creek Development has secured $31.4 million of bridge debt to refinance a single-tenant office complex in suburban Phoenix, Commercial Observer has learned Obra Real Estate provided the loan for the developer’s 2021-built office property at 1715 West Elliot Road in Gilbert, Ariz. The complex is fully leased to Northrop Grumman, a global aerospace and defense technology company. “This transaction reflects our continued focus on high-quality assets supported by institutional credit and experienced sponsorship,” Michael Bachenheimer, managing director at Obra Real Estate, said in a statement. “Our ability to move quickly and deliver certainty of close is a key differentiator, particularly for sponsors seeking flexible capital in today’s market.” Silver Creek acquired the 120,294-square-foot office building for $43.1 million from SunCap Property Group in early 2021, AZ Big Media previously reported. Arrow Real Estate Advisors arranged the transaction with a team consisting Morris Betesh, Eliott Zeitoune, Alex Bailkin and Andrew Rosenberg. Located 20 miles southeast of Downtown Phoenix within the 63-acre Gilbert Spectrum Business Park, the Northrop Grumman-leased property is strongly positioned from having a long-term tenant in place in one of the nation’s strongest-performing markets for corporate investment and employment growth, according to Arrow “This transaction underscores the continued demand for high-quality assets backed by institutional-caliber tenancy and experienced sponsorship,” Betesh, managing partner at Arrow Real Estate Advisors, said in a statement. “Northrop Grumman’s investment-grade profile, combined with the sponsor’s track record and active presence in the market, made this an exceptionally compelling finance opportunity that closed in under 30 days.” Silver Creek Development did not immediately return a request for comment. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Obra Real Estate provides $31.4M bridge refinance to Silver Creek Development on Northrop Grumman-leased Gilbert, AZ office
120,294-SF single-tenant property built 2021, fully leased to Northrop Grumman at time of refi
Silver Creek acquired from SunCap for $43.1M in early 2021; refi reflects strong defense-contractor tenancy
stories.summary: Silver Creek Development secured a $31.4 million bridge refinance from Obra Real Estate on a fully-leased, 120,294-square-foot office building in Gilbert, Arizona, leased to Northrop Grumman, a global aerospace and defense contractor. Silver Creek had acquired the 2021-built property from SunCap for $43.1 million in early 2021. The refi signals confidence in Northrop's credit and the property's long-term stability.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily development. However, note: Northrop Grumman's presence in Phoenix-area suburbs (Gilbert) and continued investment in defense tech/aerospace is a secondary demand signal. Large defense contractors attract engineering and skilled trades workers; monitor Northrop's headcount and real estate roadmap in Arizona for downstream residential demand (workforce housing, apartments near tech centers). Phoenix's west and east corridors (I-10, I-60) likely see parallel job growth.

stories.title: Manhattan’s Office Leasing Reaches Velocity Not Seen Since 2002: Report
stories.excerpt: Manhattan’s office leasing in the first half of 2026 was one for the books. The past two quarters counted just under 23 million square feet of office leases, according to Colliers’ second-quarter office report, in what the brokerage called the strongest first-half period of leasing velocity since 2002. “You really saw recovery show itself in multiple ways,” Franklin Wallach, Colliers’ executive managing director for research and business, told Commercial Observer, “On the demand side, the supply side, the pricing.” The results are music to New York City office landlords’ ears. Demand outpaced tightening supply, law firms and tech gobbled up space, and average asking rents sharply increased. Leasing volume during the second quarter totaled 11.02 million square feet, with a positive net absorption of 3.51 million square feet. That leasing activity was led by law firm Simpson Thacher & Bartlett’s 916,000-square-foot lease at Extell Development’s office development at 570 Fifth Avenue in May. Manhattan’s law firms, with their insatiable appetite for office, occupied five of the top 10 largest lease deals in the quarter, according to Wallach. Those heavyweights included Cleary Gottlieb Steen & Hamilton’s 475,000-square-foot deal at One Liberty Plaza and Alston & Bird’s 169,664-square-foot lease at 51 West 52nd Street. Average office asking rents increased to $78.03 per square foot, up 5.7 percent annually. According to Colliers, that’s the sharpest midyear annual growth since 2016. While the city at large is still working its way back to pre-pandemic standards, asking rent along high-demand corridors like Midtown’s Park Avenue have 2020 in the rearview mirror. Average rents along the avenue jumped 4.6 percent over the quarter, to $119.62 per square foot, in a nearly 14 percent increase from March 2020. Manhattan’s Class A office market commanded $92.19 per square foot last quarter, according to Savills’ second-quarter report, with an overall availability rate
bullets: Manhattan office leasing H1 2026: 23M SF leased; strongest first-half velocity since 2002
Q2 leasing 11.02M SF; net absorption +3.51M SF; Simpson Thacher & Bartlett 916K-SF lease at Extell's 570 Fifth Avenue
Law firms and tech companies driving demand; rents rising sharply across all submarkets
stories.summary: Manhattan office leasing in H1 2026 totaled just under 23 million square feet, marking the strongest first-half leasing velocity since 2002, per Colliers. Q2 activity reached 11.02 million square feet with positive net absorption of 3.51 million square feet. Law firms (Simpson Thacher & Bartlett's 916K-SF deal at Extell's 570 Fifth Avenue) and tech companies are the primary drivers; average asking rents are rising sharply across demand and supply sides.
stories.impact: Strong office demand in Manhattan suggests corporate presence and job stability, but creates minimal direct demand for new multifamily because office markets and resi markets have diverged. However, the influx of law and tech talent into Manhattan may support pricing and absorption in adjacent residential markets (especially waterfront, Tribeca, Midtown South, Long Island City). For TCR: focus on converting Class B/C office to apartments (as GFP Development is doing at 25 Water Street and 222 Broadway) rather than expecting new demand from office leasing; the office recovery is real but won't drive net new resi supply as efficiently as regulatory-driven conversions.

stories.title: Miami’s Office Leasing Jumps Nearly 45% in Q2
stories.excerpt: Miami-Dade County’s office market remains strong as companies compete for premium office space, pushing rents to record highs as vacancy continues to tighten. Availability fell to 14.9 percent, down from 17.1 percent a year ago, during the second quarter of 2026, according to data from Savills. Leasing activity jumped to 1.3 million square feet, increasing by nearly 45 percent year-over-year. The average asking rent climbed to $67.12 per square foot, up 7 percent from a year earlier and nearly 57 percent higher than five years ago. The Airport West, Downtown Miami and Coral Gables submarkets accounted for the largest share of leasing activity. Some large transactions included cafe chain Pura Vida relocating its corporate office from Sunset Harbour to the Gateway at Wynwood building, where it leased 26,982 square feet, as well as Planet Fitness taking 26,207 square feet within the Omni Center at 1501 Biscayne Boulevard. Family offices, financial firms, technology companies, law firms — many of them new to the Miami-Dade market — are driving the bulk of the demand at the top end of the market. Miami Beach and Coconut Grove — sought-after residential neighborhoods, where well-paid executives typically live — also boasts the county’s most expensive office rents, which average roughly $99 per square foot. Countywide, rents for Class A buildings rose by 6 percent year-over-year to $80.85 a square foot, reflecting demand for new, amenity-rich properties. Just this month, the family office of billionaire Peter Thiel, a PayPal co-founder and early Facebook investor, signed an 18,158-square-foot lease at Miami’s 830 Brickell, paying $250 per square foot, according to Bloomberg. The price is Miami-Dade County’s most expensive office lease, rivaling rents commanded by some of Manhattan’s premier office towers. Developers of new, luxury buildings are taking note. The office space at the Well, Terra’s mixed-use development in Bay Harbor Islands, is being markete
bullets: Miami-Dade Q2 2026 office leasing jumped 45% YoY to 1.3M SF; vacancy fell to 14.9% from 17.1%
Average asking rent $67.12/SF, up 7% YoY, up 57% over 5 years; Pura Vida, Planet Fitness among major recent movers
Airport West, Downtown Miami, Coral Gables driving activity; family offices, fintech, law firms entering market
stories.summary: Miami-Dade County's office leasing activity surged nearly 45% year-over-year in Q2 2026 to 1.3 million square feet, with vacancy tightening to 14.9% from 17.1% a year earlier. Average asking rents climbed to $67.12 per square foot (up 7% YoY, 57% in five years). Large tenants include Pura Vida (relocating corporate HQ, 26,982 SF) and Planet Fitness (26,207 SF); family offices, fintech, and law firms are expanding into Miami-Dade.
stories.impact: Strong office demand in Miami-Dade signals job growth and corporate relocation momentum (especially fintech and family office clusters in Brickell/Coral Gables). This creates secondary residential demand: professionals and support staff linked to these firms require housing. For TCR: monitor employer relocations and headcount plans in Miami's fintech/legal/family office hubs; concurrent with office leasing strength, multifamily delivery in Brickell, Downtown Miami, and Wynwood should see strong absorption and rent growth. The 57% rent growth over five years suggests pricing power for new construction in this market.

stories.title: Victor Sigoura on how to be a NYC condo developer
stories.excerpt: Victor Sigoura’s first gig at a development firm was working on one of the city’s most iconic and controversial projects.  Sigoura left his job as a lawyer at a white shoe firm to work for El-Ad Group, which had just acquired The Plaza and planned to convert a portion of the hotel into luxury condos. The hotel union fought the conversion, leading to a dramatic escalation. “I had the pleasure of handling the union. I used to get beat up by them every single day, like yelling matches,” said Sigoura, who said El-Ad’s founder Isaac Tshuva and then CEO Miki […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Victor Sigoura (Legion Investment Group CEO) converts NYC office towers: 25 Water (1,300 units), 222 Broadway (788 units), 100 Gold Street (3,700 units)
Legion's first project (32-unit condo, 109 East 79th St) sold at $4K+/SF; current pipeline includes 30-story West Village condo, 20-story Gramercy Park project
GFP Development spun out in April 2026 under Sigoura; family legacy (Gural real estate dynasty dating to 1950s office-to-resi conversions)
stories.summary: Victor Sigoura, CEO of GFP Development (spun from GFP Real Estate in April 2026), is executing NYC's largest office-to-resi conversion pipeline: 25 Water Street (1,300 units), 222 Broadway (788 units), and 100 Gold Street (3,700 units, city-selected). Legion Investment Group, Sigoura's earlier firm, achieved strong pricing on smaller luxury condo projects ($4K+/SF); new pipeline includes mid-sized condo projects in West Village and Gramercy Park.
stories.impact: This is a flagship signal for office-to-resi conversion economics in NYC. GFP's 5,788-unit pipeline (plus city-selected 3,700 at 100 Gold) demonstrates that regulatory support, construction economics, and market demand now favor conversion over traditional construction. For TCR: office-to-resi is a viable supply source in NYC and should be considered as an alternative to greenfield or retail redevelopment; conversion timelines and cost are favorable if zoning and financing align. Monitor GFP's delivery pace, pricing, and absorption rates—these will set comps for other NYC developers and lenders evaluating conversion feasibility.

stories.title: Starwood Capital Group Closes $10.2B Opportunistic Fund for Data Center Investment
stories.excerpt: Barry Sternlicht wants an even bigger piece of the expanding data center pie. Sternlicht’s Starwood Capital Group announced Wednesday that it closed a $10.2 billion opportunistic real estate fund to invest in residential, industrial, hospitality and data center real estate. The fund will target mainly deals in the U.S. and Europe, but will also look into Asia. Bloomberg first reported the news of the closing. Starwood envisions the fund — known as Starwood Distressed Opportunity Fund XIII — will double its previous commitment to data centers and deploy a full 35 percent of the opportunistic capital toward an asset class that now powers the AI revolution, according to Sternlicht, the firm’s chairman and founder. “We are excited about the opportunities we have already sourced for this fund and are proud of our track record of delivering results for our investors through market cycles,” said Sternlicht in a statement. Starwood’s strategy, at least on the data center front, will focus on co-investment deals with larger players to bridge the huge capital needs most data center development require, which can reach as much as $2 billion for stand-alone assets. Other approaches will see the firm inject equity or debt into developments on a scattershot basis. Jonathan Pollack, president of Starwood Capital, said in a statement that his firm has seen investment tailwinds due to a lack of supply across traditional asset classes such as multifamily, hospitality and industrial, as well as “a tremendous growth” in technological and industrial assets. “This is an exciting time to be investing in real estate,” he added. Starwood Distressed Opportunity Fund XIII has already closed 20 transactions totaling $3 billion of equity. The firm has teamed up with MARA Holdings, a vertically integrated digital energy and infrastructure company, to convert former bitcoin mining sites into new data center developments. Starwood Capital Group has roughly $130 billion of assets under manage
bullets: Starwood Capital Group closes $10.2B opportunistic fund (Fund XIII); 35% allocated to data centers (doubling prior commitment)
Fund targets U.S., Europe, Asia; focuses on residential, industrial, hospitality, and data center real estate
Barry Sternlicht strategy includes co-investments with larger data center players to bridge capital and operational gaps
stories.summary: Starwood Capital Group announced the close of a $10.2 billion opportunistic real estate fund (Distressed Opportunity Fund XIII) with 35% of dry powder allocated to data centers, doubling its prior data center commitment. The fund will invest in residential, industrial, hospitality, and data center assets across the U.S., Europe, and Asia. Starwood's data center strategy focuses on co-investment deals with larger institutional players to scale exposure.
stories.impact: Data center capital dominance (35% of a $10.2B fund) indicates major institutional conviction in AI/cloud infrastructure demand. Indirectly: data center clusters (hyperscale campuses, edge facilities) drive workforce and utility/power infrastructure demand in supporting regions; e.g., Phoenix, Northern Virginia, Texas. For TCR: monitor which metros/corridors attract Starwood's data center investments, then underwrite workforce housing in those submarkets (electricians, technicians, engineers). A large data center campus may support 200–500 direct and indirect jobs, validating 400–800-unit apartment communities in secondary and tertiary markets.

stories.title: NYC Closes on $214M Financing for 228-Unit Affordable Bronx Senior Housing Project
stories.excerpt: More affordable senior housing is coming to the Bronx. New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani announced Wednesday morning the financial closing for Sol on Park, a $214 million, 228-unit affordable senior housing development project coming to the Morris Houses campus in the Bronx’s Claremont Village neighborhood. To be built at 3728 Park Avenue, Sol on Park is being developed through a partnership between the New York City Housing Authority (NYCHA), the Department of Housing Preservation and Development (HPD) and the Housing Development Corporation (HDC). It’s unclear who provided the financing for the project. A spokesperson for NYCHA did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Once completed, Sol on Park will include 80 apartments reserved for current NYCHA residents living at the Morris Houses campus, 68 homes for formerly homeless seniors, and 80 units that will be made available through the Housing Connect lottery for low- and middle-income seniors. The project also includes a community space and a new pedestrian plaza. “New Yorkers need housing relief, and we’re using every tool at our disposal to deliver it,” Mamdani said in a statement. “This project shows what is possible when city agencies work together to put public land and public investment to work for the people of this city. We are building affordable homes, creating opportunities for NYCHA residents and proving that the government can act with the urgency this housing crisis demands.” The project is also set to include on-site services from local health centers, as well as bike storage, laundry facilities, exercise rooms and computer labs, according to the announcement. The city expects residents to begin moving into Sol on Park in early 2029. “As we strive to ensure safe, affordable housing for all New Yorkers, we must also make sure that our most vulnerable residents are cared for,” Dina Levy, HPD commissioner, said in a statement. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobse
bullets: NYC closes $214M financing for Sol on Park, 228-unit affordable senior housing in Bronx's Claremont Village
Partnership: NYCHA, HPD, HDC; 80 units for current NYCHA residents, 68 for formerly homeless seniors, 80 via Housing Connect lottery
3728 Park Avenue location; financing source not immediately disclosed; development team Magnusson Architecture and Planning
stories.summary: New York City achieved financial closing for Sol on Park, a $214 million, 228-unit affordable senior housing project on the Morris Houses NYCHA campus in the Bronx's Claremont Village. The project includes 80 units for current NYCHA residents, 68 for formerly homeless seniors, and 80 for low-income households via Housing Connect. Development is a tri-agency partnership (NYCHA, HPD, HDC) with Magnusson Architecture and Planning as design lead.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on market-rate multifamily, but signals strong public capital and political will for senior and affordable housing in NYC's outer boroughs. For TCR: monitor NYC's HPD financing programs and NYCHA modernization schedule (potential ground leases, partnerships, or adjacent development sites). The Bronx remains significantly undersupplied in new market-rate apartments; if Sol on Park stabilizes and rents its units, adjacent market-rate projects may benefit from improved neighborhood amenities and perception.

stories.title: Texas developers thought a state law to expand as-of-right housing production in commercial zones would help them build
stories.excerpt: The Texas legislature’s new law seemed a pro-housing dream. Passed last year, it requires larger municipalities to allow mixed-use and multifamily development in zones that permit office, commercial, retail or warehouse uses. Yet some developers say the law, Senate Bill 840, meant to emancipate builders from restrictions, can’t streamline pre-development negotiations with city officials — some of whom consider the law an encroachment on local authority. “Just because something can be done legally doesn’t mean that things can’t be stalled in the background with all the bureaucracy,” Tanya Ragan, founder of adaptive reuse development company Wildcat Management, said. It’s less […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Texas SB 840 law (2025) allows mixed-use/multifamily in commercial zones; Arlington, Plano, Irving, Frisco passed local ordinances to limit/circumvent
Cities instituted height caps, required expensive amenities, rezoned commercial to heavy industrial to exploit exceptions
Developers report state law creates theoretical opportunity but practical delays; political opposition and bureaucratic friction persist
stories.summary: Texas Senate Bill 840, passed in 2025 and effective September 1, 2025, requires larger municipalities to allow mixed-use and multifamily development in commercial/retail/office zones. However, multiple North Texas cities (Arlington, Plano, Irving, Frisco) pre-emptively passed ordinances imposing height limits, costly amenity requirements, and industrial zoning changes to circumvent the state law's intent. Developers report the law hasn't streamlined approvals; political negotiations remain arduous.
stories.impact: SB 840 is a landmark pro-housing statute, but local resistance in North Texas is eroding its practical impact. For TCR: expect protracted permitting even where SB 840 technically applies; budget 12–18 months for approvals in Plano, Frisco, and Irving versus 6–9 months in more permitting-friendly markets (Austin, Dallas proper). Consider secondary/tertiary Texas markets (San Antonio, Lubbock, Corpus Christi) where local governments are less organized to block state law, or focus on Dallas proper where city government is more neutral. SB 840 upside requires activist investment in city relations and possibly litigation.

stories.title: The Closing: Brian Steinwurtzel
stories.excerpt: Brian Steinwurtzel’s grandfather built a real estate dynasty turning old West Side warehouses into office buildings.  Some seven decades later, Steinwurtzel is following the family tradition by converting outdated office buildings into apartments. The 49-year-old has been heading up GFP Real Estate’s developments for the past 13 years. In April, the Gural family spun out the resi-conversion side of the business into GFP Development, naming Steinwurtzel the new entity’s CEO. In recent years, he’s run projects like SoMA — the conversion of the Brutalist-styled 25 Water Street office building into 1,300 rental apartments. He’s also turning the 31-story office tower […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Brian Steinwurtzel (GFP Development CEO) heads $214M+ office-to-apartment conversion pipeline: 25 Water St (1,300 units), 222 Broadway (788 units), 100 Gold St (3,700 units)
GFP spun as separate entity in April 2026; family legacy: Gural dynasty (grandfather Aaron built Newmark, bought Flatiron, started 1950s office conversions)
SoMA and 222 Broadway exemplify NYC conversion model; Flatiron condo conversion in progress with Brodsky Organization
stories.summary: Brian Steinwurtzel, newly appointed CEO of GFP Development (spun from GFP Real Estate in April 2026), is managing a multi-billion-dollar office-to-apartment conversion pipeline including SoMA (25 Water Street, 1,300 units), 222 Broadway (788 units), and NYC's largest single project, 100 Gold Street (3,700 units). The Gural family real estate legacy dates to Aaron Gural's 1950s office-to-residential conversions; current projects include the Flatiron Building conversion (with Brodsky Organization) and mixed-use developments in prime Manhattan submarkets.
stories.impact: This profile reinforces NYC office-to-resi conversion as a dominant development strategy and a generational wealth and expertise play. For TCR: GFP's execution track record (SoMA, 222 Broadway) and Steinwurtzel's leadership demonstrate that conversion feasibility, financing, and leasing economics are now mature. The scale of the 100 Gold Street project (3,700 units) and Flatiron's symbolism suggest that even trophy office assets can be converted, expanding the supply pipeline significantly. Monitor GFP's delivery, pricing, and absorption metrics as bellwethers for NYC market tightening and conversion ROI.

stories.title: Hamptons developer Jeremy Morton’s unfinished projects — and debts — stack up
stories.excerpt: You wouldn’t be surprised to meet Jeremy Morton in the Hamptons. Tall and ruddy, Morton rented an apartment in the West Village and owned a 12,800-square-foot home in East Hampton with an infinity pool, pool house and at-home gym on 2 acres.  He married his wife, Brooke, at a black-tie wedding at the Metropolitan Club. She would post about the couple’s lifestyle to her travel blog and Instagram account, both called the Glowtrotter, with pictures detailing their dozens of trips to places like Burning Man, Greece and St. Tropez.  Undergirding the conspicuous consumption was a growing Hamptons real estate portfolio. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Jeremy Morton (Hamptons developer) acquired $30M Sag Harbor property (2 Main St/22 Long Island Ave) in 2025; highest retail price ever paid in Hamptons
Morton's portfolio includes custom homes, Ruschmeyer's nightclub, Cyril's Fish House, Rick's Crabby Cowboy, retail in Southampton and Water Mill
Multiple partners report unfinished projects and mounting debts; Morton's lifestyle supported by expanding portfolio
stories.summary: Jeremy Morton, a Hamptons real estate developer and lifestyle investor, paid a record $30 million in 2025 for a 22,000-square-foot corner property at 2 Main Street and 22 Long Island Avenue in Sag Harbor. His portfolio includes custom homes, the nightclub Ruschmeyer's, casual dining (Cyril's Fish House, Rick's Crabby Cowboy), and retail holdings across Water Mill and Southampton. Multiple partners report unfinished projects and growing debts underpinning Morton's portfolio.
stories.impact: This is a cautionary tale on speculative overreach in a luxury secondary market, not a multifamily-specific signal. However, note: the Hamptons' record-high retail pricing ($30M for a corner parcel) reflects strong demand and capital deployment in seasonal markets. For TCR: Hamptons and East End markets are not core multifamily zones (regulatory resistance to density, seasonal economy), but the high capital and valuation pressure could create distressed asset opportunities if developers like Morton unwind. Monitor Morton's portfolio for eventual sales/auctions; secondary Hamptons markets (Montauk, Amagansett) may present value-add or stabilized acquisition opportunities if credit tightens.
